transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hello, Leo. I’m glad you’re here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Um, I guess I’m not really sure. There’s not much going on, you know? Just the usual stuff at work and home. I mean, I had a good moment at work recently, but I don’t really want to get into it. It’s not like it matters much.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re feeling a bit uncertain about what to focus on today. You mentioned having a good moment at work, but it seems like you’re hesitant to dive into that. I wonder if there’s something about that moment that feels significant to you, even if it doesn’t seem like it matters much. What do you think?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I guess it was nice to get a bonus for my performance. But then, when I told my parents, they just asked if I was looking for a new job. It felt like they didn’t really get what I was excited about. I don’t know, maybe I just don’t want to feel judged or something. It’s easier to keep things to myself.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like receiving the bonus was a positive experience for you, but sharing it with your parents didn’t go as you hoped. Their response made you feel judged, which seems to have dampened your excitement. I can understand why you might feel it’s easier to keep things to yourself in that case. How did their reaction make you feel about sharing your achievements in the future?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Honestly, it makes me think twice about sharing anything like that again. I mean, if they’re just going to focus on what’s next or whether I should be looking for something better, it feels like I can’t really celebrate what I’ve done. It’s just... disappointing, I guess. I don’t want to feel vulnerable or like I’m not good enough, you know? So, it’s easier to just not say anything.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds really disappointing to feel like you can’t celebrate your achievements with your parents without facing that kind of scrutiny. It makes sense that you’d want to protect yourself from feeling vulnerable or inadequate. Keeping things to yourself might feel safer, but I wonder how that impacts your sense of accomplishment or connection with others. What do you think about that?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, I guess it does affect how I feel about my accomplishments. It’s like, if I can’t share it, then it almost feels like it didn’t happen. But at the same time, I don’t want to deal with the pressure of their expectations or judgments. It’s just... easier to keep it to myself. I don’t know if that’s the best way to handle it, but it feels like the safest option right now.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It makes sense that you’d feel that way. Keeping your accomplishments to yourself can feel like a way to protect against the pressure of expectations and judgments, even if it also diminishes the joy of those moments. It’s a tough balance to strike. I wonder if there are other people in your life with whom you might feel more comfortable sharing your successes. Have you thought about that?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I mean, I guess there are a couple of friends I could talk to, but I’m not sure they’d really get it either. Sometimes it feels like nobody really understands what I go through. I don’t want to burden anyone with my stuff. So, I just keep it in. It’s just... easier that way, I think.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re feeling a sense of isolation, as if you’re carrying these experiences alone. You mentioned that it feels like nobody really understands what you go through, which can be really heavy. It’s understandable to worry about burdening others, but it also seems like keeping everything inside might be weighing on you. What do you think holds you back from reaching out to those friends, even if it feels easier to keep things to yourself?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I guess I just don’t want to come off as needy or like I’m looking for attention. It feels like everyone has their own stuff to deal with, and I don’t want to add to that. Plus, I’m not really good at opening up. It’s hard to find the right words, and I worry they won’t really get it anyway. So, it just seems simpler to stay quiet.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re really mindful of how your feelings might impact others, which shows a lot of care. I can see how that concern, combined with the difficulty of finding the right words, makes it hard to open up. It’s understandable to want to avoid feeling needy or like you’re seeking attention. I wonder if there’s a part of you that feels it would be helpful to share, even if it’s just a little bit, or if you feel more comfortable keeping things to yourself for now. What do you think?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I mean, part of me thinks it could be helpful to share, just to get it off my chest. But then I worry about how it’ll be received. What if they don’t respond the way I hope? Or what if it just makes things awkward? It feels safer to keep things to myself, even if it’s not the best option. I guess I’m just stuck in that mindset right now.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re caught in a tough spot between wanting to share and the fear of how it might be received. That worry about potential awkwardness or not getting the response you hope for can really hold you back. It’s understandable to feel safer keeping things to yourself, especially when you’re unsure of how others will react. I wonder if there’s a small step you could take that feels a bit less daunting, like sharing a little something with a friend or even just writing it down for yourself. What do you think about that?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I suppose writing it down could be a start. It feels a bit less risky than talking to someone directly. I could just get my thoughts out without worrying about how someone else might react. But then again, I wonder if it would really help. I mean, it’s just me and my thoughts, and sometimes they feel pretty heavy. I don’t know if writing would change anything, but it might be worth a try.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like writing could be a helpful way to express those heavy thoughts without the pressure of someone else's reaction. It’s understandable to question whether it will make a difference, but sometimes just getting things out on paper can provide a sense of relief or clarity. You don’t have to commit to anything big; even jotting down a few lines might help you process what you’re feeling. How do you feel about giving that a try, even if it’s just a small step?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, I think I could try that. Just writing a few lines might help me sort through what I’m feeling without the pressure of sharing it with someone right away. It feels like a manageable step, at least. I guess I’ll give it a shot and see if it helps clear my mind a bit. Thanks for suggesting it.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: I’m glad to hear that feels manageable for you. Writing can be a powerful way to explore your thoughts and feelings at your own pace. It’s great that you’re open to trying it out. Remember, there’s no right or wrong way to do it—just let your thoughts flow. If you ever want to share how it went or talk more about what you wrote, I’m here for that too. How are you feeling about taking this step?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I feel a bit relieved, actually. It’s nice to have a way to express things without the pressure of a conversation. I’m still a little anxious about what I might write, but I think it could help me sort through my thoughts. It’s good to know that I can share if I want to later on. I appreciate you being here for that.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: I’m really glad to hear you’re feeling a bit relieved. It’s completely normal to feel some anxiety about what you might write, but it sounds like you’re approaching it with an open mind. Having that option to share later if you choose can provide some comfort too. Remember, it’s all about what feels right for you. If you ever want to talk more about your writing or anything else, I’m here to support you. Is there anything else on your mind today, or are you feeling good about wrapping up for now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think I’m okay with wrapping up for now. I feel like I have a plan with the writing, and that’s a good step for me. I appreciate the support and understanding. I’ll see how it goes and maybe we can talk about it next time. Thanks for listening.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You’re very welcome, Leo. I’m glad you feel like you have a plan, and it’s great that you’re taking this step for yourself. I look forward to hearing how it goes whenever you’re ready to share. Take care of yourself, and remember that it’s okay to take things at your own pace. Have a good day!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you. I appreciate that. I’ll take care, and I’ll see you next time. Have a good day too!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You’re welcome, Leo. I’m glad we could talk today. Take care, and I look forward to our next conversation. Have a wonderful day!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thanks! I’m looking forward to it too. Take care and have a great day!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Thank you, Leo! I appreciate that. Take care, and I’ll see you next time!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: You too! See you next time!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: See you next time, Leo! Take care!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: See you! Take care!

This line of questioning began to shift the client's perspective on their experiences and feelings of judgment, indicating a move toward higher-level self-exploration. However, while the questions were effective in facilitating deeper thought, they did not reach the level of challenging assumptions or revealing hidden motivations to the extent that would warrant a higher rating.

The client’s most recent interactions reveal a notable increase in emotional expression and self-reflection.
